narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 and LSAT score of 29.50. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicts a 76.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for law school admission was rejected—schools use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed on the bar. This high failure probability stems from several key aspects of your academic record that stood out to the model as stronger warning signs compared to typical applicants. Most importantly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—well below the average of around 5.5—pushed the prediction heavily toward failure, signaling consistently low performance in later undergraduate years relative to your peers. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also much lower than the typical 5.7 average, reinforced this concern as the next biggest factor dragging the odds down. While your LSAT score, though below the average of 36.7, actually worked slightly in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, it wasn't enough to offset those early and persistent grade deciles. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, lower than the dataset average of 3.21, added further weight toward the failure prediction, unlike your family income bracket, which had a minor negative influence. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your full-time enrollment plans helped a bit, aligning with the vast majority (over 92%) who succeed that way, but your overall profile places you in a riskier group academically. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a solid background—many in similar situations improve with targeted effort. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and perhaps gaining relevant experience to rebuild your academic narrative. With these steps, you could reapply stronger next cycle and shift the model's view in your favor. Hang in there; persistence pays off for determined applicants like you.
